title: Evaluating Gender Bias in Natural Language Inference

structured.summary: This paper proposes a novel challenge task to detect gender bias in Natural Language Inference (NLI) models by pairing gender-neutral premises with gender-specific hypotheses differing only in gender. It evaluates state-of-the-art transformer-based NLI models fine-tuned on MNLI and SNLI, revealing significant occupational gender bias aligned with real-world stereotypes, and demonstrates that simple gender-swapping data augmentation can reduce bias without harming accuracy.

strengths.point: Novel evaluation methodology for gender bias in NLI
strengths.evidence: The paper introduces a paired premise-hypothesis challenge task using gender-neutral premises and gender-specific hypotheses to isolate gender bias effects.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical analysis across multiple models and datasets
strengths.evidence: Evaluations include BERT, RoBERTa, and BART fine-tuned on MNLI and SNLI, tested in-distribution and out-of-distribution (ANLI, QNLI), with bias metrics correlated to real-world occupational statistics.

strengths.point: Effective debiasing via simple gender-swapping data augmentation
strengths.evidence: Augmentation reduces bias metrics significantly for BERT with negligible impact on accuracy, highlighting a practical mitigation strategy.

weaknesses.evidence: No reported human annotation or automated checks to confirm premises are truly free of residual gender cues, risking confounded bias measurement.

weaknesses.point: Limited ecological validity due to simplistic hypothesis templates
weaknesses.evidence: Hypotheses use formulaic templates like 'This text talks about a [gender] occupation,' which may exaggerate bias detection and lack natural language complexity.

weaknesses.point: Lack of statistical significance testing and calibration analysis
weaknesses.evidence: Bias metrics lack confidence intervals or p-values, and no assessment of model calibration is provided despite reliance on probability-based metrics.

weaknesses.point: Narrow focus on occupational gender bias without intersectional or broader gendered terms
weaknesses.evidence: Study excludes other gendered words (e.g., names) and intersectional identities, limiting scope and real-world applicability.

weaknesses.point: Missing detailed reporting of experimental settings and reproducibility information
weaknesses.evidence: Key hyperparameters, training details, and data augmentation ratios are not disclosed, hindering replication.

questions.question: How robust are the bias metrics to variations in hypothesis phrasing beyond simple templates?
questions.motivation: Understanding if bias detection generalizes to more naturalistic language is critical for real-world applicability.

questions.question: To what extent does pretraining data contribute to observed gender bias compared to fine-tuning datasets?
questions.motivation: Disentangling bias sources can guide more targeted debiasing strategies.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient validation of gender-neutral premises. No reported human annotation or automated checks to confirm premises are truly free of residual gender cues, risking confounded bias measurement
critique_points.point_002: Limited ecological validity due to simplistic hypothesis templates. Hypotheses use formulaic templates like 'This text talks about a [gender] occupation,' which may exaggerate bias detection and lack natural language complexity
critique_points.point_003: Lack of statistical significance testing and calibration analysis. Bias metrics lack confidence intervals or p-values, and no assessment of model calibration is provided despite reliance on probability-based metrics
critique_points.point_004: Narrow focus on occupational gender bias without intersectional or broader gendered terms. Study excludes other gendered words (e.g., names) and intersectional identities, limiting scope and real-world applicability
critique_points.point_005: Missing detailed reporting of experimental settings and reproducibility information. Key hyperparameters, training details, and data augmentation ratios are not disclosed, hindering replication
